summary: Admits expert testimony resting on reliable principles reliably applied to the facts — the rule entomological PMI testimony is screened under after Daubert and Kumho Tire.
Authorized Privy Council orders to prevent introduction and spread of Colorado beetle, including landing prohibitions, crop destruction, entry on land, recordkeeping, and penalties.
Converted insect-killing chemistry into a federal commerce problem by barring adulterated or misbranded insecticides, Paris greens, lead arsenates, and fungicides in interstate trade.

Created a dedicated federal plant-quarantine apparatus for plant diseases and insect pests, including import exclusion, interstate quarantine, inspection, disinfection, certification, and a Federal Horticultural Board with Bureau of Entomology participation.

short_title: India Destructive Insects and Pests Act
summary: Gave central authority to prevent introduction into India and interprovincial transport of insects, fungi, or other pests destructive to crops.
The master statute empowering USDA APHIS to prohibit movement, declare quarantines, require permits, and seize infested articles; civil penalties reach $1,000,000 per willful violation.
A strict-liability criminal statute that conspicuously does not list insects, deferring to the Plant Protection Act for plant pests and leaving non-plant-pest insects in a regulatory gap.
Active federal-coordinated quarantine for Lycorma delicatula across numerous states.
Active eradication quarantine for Anoplophora glabripennis.
Lists priority quarantine insects and operates a plant-passport system across the Union.
Creates the Union list of invasive alien species of Union concern, whose listed species face restrictions on keeping, importing, selling, breeding, growing, and release.
The treaty whose ISPMs set the phytosanitary benchmark used under the WTO SPS Agreement to judge whether a quarantine is science-based or a disguised trade barrier.
cross_references: Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (1995)

citation: Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (1995)
summary: Requires that phytosanitary insect-quarantine measures be science-based and not disguised restrictions on trade.
Defines protected wildlife to expressly include any arthropod or other invertebrate, putting non-pest insects within the statutory eligibility frame while retaining an express § 3(6) pest carve-out.
Defines 'fish' to include invertebrates — the hook on which Almond Alliance held that bumblebees are 'fish' eligible for CESA listing.

short_title: Colorado invertebrate and rare-plant conservation authority
summary: Adds rare plants and invertebrates to Colorado's nongame conservation law and authorizes voluntary programs to conserve, protect, and perpetuate invertebrates.

citation: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (1973)
summary: Lists certain insects — notably birdwing butterflies on Appendix I/II — and requires domestic implementing legislation to make trade legal, sustainable, and traceable.
Sets the current global biodiversity target architecture into which insect-conservation indicators and national restoration duties must fit.
Makes pollinator decline a restoration-law target, tying insect conservation to EU-wide monitoring and reversal duties.
Restricts importation of honey bees; states layer apiary registration, disease-control, and Africanized-bee rules on top.
State controls on Africanized honey bees within California's billion-dollar almond-pollination economy.

citation: Virginia Act Concerning Planting of Mulberry Trees (1658)
summary: Required land proprietors to plant and tend mulberry trees, making the plant infrastructure for silkworm production a statutory development duty.

citation: Virginia Act Concerning Mulberry Trees and Silke-Flaxe (1619)
summary: Required settled colonists to plant and maintain mulberry trees and to plant silk-flax, making silk inputs an early statutory development duty.
The provision under which Chakrabarty and Ex parte Allen made engineered insect lines and their products patentable.

citation: Coordinated Framework for Regulation of Biotechnology (1986)
summary: Splits jurisdiction over genetically modified insects among EPA (FIFRA), USDA (APHIS), and FDA — the regime under which Oxitec's engineered Aedes aegypti was released in the Florida Keys.
The international biosafety frame relevant to gene-drive insects, even though deployment-specific governance remains thin.
Establishes sovereign rights over genetic resources and a benefit-sharing duty — directly relevant to bioprospecting insect venom peptides, antimicrobials, and silk proteins.
Treats insects as novel foods requiring pre-market EFSA safety assessment; has authorized house cricket, yellow mealworm, migratory locust, and others through individual implementing regulations.

Deems food adulterated if it consists in part of any filthy substance — the provision behind FDA insect-fragment defect action levels, between which edible insects sit without a dedicated US approval pathway.

short_title: Utah cultivated meat and insect-based substitute labeling
summary: Requires consumer-facing labeling for food containing plant or insect-based meat substitutes, making insect protein a named state-law product category.
Relaxed the post-BSE feed ban to admit processed insect protein into aquaculture feed (extended to poultry and pigs in 2021).

Recognizes animal sentience and covers decapod crustaceans and cephalopods; § 5(2) reserves a regulatory power to extend coverage to any invertebrate — insects included — without new primary legislation.
The key instrument governing animal use in scientific procedures; it systematically excludes insects despite growing sentience evidence.

citation: Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Art. 13
summary: Acknowledges animals as sentient beings, but the clause is not self-executing and in practice protections reach almost exclusively vertebrates.
Declares organized public programs the best protection against vector-borne disease and creates special districts to surveil and control mosquitoes; California has run such districts since 1915.

citation: Strengthening Mosquito Abatement for Safety and Health Act of 2018
summary: House-passed authorization of mosquito-control funding and CDC epidemiology-laboratory capacity grants — federal recognition of mosquito biology as national public-health law.
Treats insect vectors as covered means of delivery under its General Purpose Criterion, though ambiguity persists over uninfected insects used in crop warfare.

citation: Agricultural Bioterrorism Protection Act of 2002, 7 U.S.C. § 8401
summary: Classifies certain plant pests and pathogens as select agents subject to stringent possession and transfer regulation.
